cataloging_subject.label: Proprium Sanctorum: Supplementary Homilies from the Vernon Manuscript (Horstmann selection)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The intellectual and editorial unit published by Carl Horstmann in Archiv 81 (1888), pp. 83-114 and 299-321, and represented as CME00064: twenty-eight complete anonymous Middle English verse homilies from the Vernon Manuscript supplementary sanctorale, six internal title-only cross-references retained from the manuscript sequence, Latin Gospel rubrics, and German editorial framing and notes. It is not one medieval poem, not the complete Northern Homily Cycle, not the entire Vernon supplementary sequence, and not the whole Vernon Manuscript.

catalog_summary.title: Proprium Sanctorum: Supplementary Homilies from the Vernon Manuscript (Horstmann selection)
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous prologue poet and anonymous homilist or homilists
catalog_summary.date: Supplementary homilies composed in the late fourteenth century; parallel Add MS 22283 witness dated 1360-1399; Vernon witness dated 1381-1400, commonly c. 1390; selection published 1888
catalog_summary.region: First Vernon/Simeon expansion transmitted in a Midland dialect; Horstmann tentatively localized the prologue to northwestern England and the remaining additions to southern England

titles.value: Proprium Sanctorum: Supplementary Homilies from the Vernon Manuscript (Horstmann selection)
notes: The qualifier makes the edited-selection boundary visible in human-readable browsing. Horstmann's exact title is retained separately.

titles.value: Proprium Sanctorum: Zusatz-Homilien des Ms. Vernon fol. CCXV ff. zur nördlichen Sammlung der Dominicalia evangelia
Sanctorale: Supplementary Homilies from Vernon Manuscript fol. 215 onward for the Northern Collection of Sunday Gospel Homilies
notes: Proprium Sanctorum is rendered descriptively as Sanctorale; fol. CCXV is Horstmann's Roman-numeral reference to fol. 215.

notes: Horstmann hypothesizes mostly one southern poet, but the record does not convert that nineteenth-century hypothesis into confirmed single authorship.

form_statements.description: The medieval content consists of 5,582 rhyming Middle English lines in sixty-four verse groups. Latin rubrics and brief German editorial prose do not make the cataloged medieval selection substantively mixed-form.

genre_statements.label: Edited selection
notes: The selection omits one full repeated manuscript homily while retaining six title-only cross-references.

scope.description: Six separately headed illustrative narratives within the Mary Magdalene homily
Horstmann's 1888 printed editorial layer
Feast-day sequence after the prologue; dedication homily modeled separately

subject_statements.label: Christ's Passion and Eucharistic bread imagery
Saint Peter and Saints Peter and Paul
Exaltation of the Holy Cross
notes: A different full Invention of the Cross homily, Erat homo, is excluded from Horstmann's selection.

subject_statements.label: Saint Matthew, Saint Michael, and Saint Luke
Doctor or confessor, possibly Saint Martin

assertion.notes: The Vos estis sal terre Gospel identifies a common rather than uniquely named feast; Horstmann tentatively associates the position with Martin.

summaries.value: A sequence of twenty-eight Middle English verse homilies edited from the Vernon Manuscript, opening with an extended grain-of-wheat meditation on Christ's Passion and Eucharistic bread and proceeding through feast-day Gospel paraphrase and exposition from the vigil of Saint Andrew to a church dedication. Horstmann's selection preserves six manuscript cross-references but omits one complete repeated Invention of the Cross homily. The Mary Magdalene homily includes six separately headed exempla.

The manuscript sequence has thirty-five feast positions: twenty-nine full homilies and six title-only pointers to homilies already present elsewhere. Horstmann prints twenty-eight of the full homilies in two journal runs and retains the pointers; he omits the repeated full Erat homo homily. The XML represents the resulting twenty-eight-homily edited unit as 5,582 lines in sixty-four verse groups.

This record describes Horstmann's selected scholarly edition and its twenty-eight constituent homilies. The Northern Homily Cycle, Vernon codex, Add MS 22283 parallel, omitted Erat homo homily, and following How a Man Shall Live Perfectly are related but distinct units.

extent.description: One cataloging root plus twenty-eight complete verse homilies. The XML contains 5,582 L elements in sixty-four LG elements. Six manuscript title-only references are embedded within the relevant homily sequence rather than counted as additional content parts. The full Erat homo manuscript homily is excluded from the edited selection.

content_parts.label: Horstmann's supplementary sanctorale selection
content_parts.summary: Twenty-eight complete verse homilies selected from a thirty-five-position Vernon feast sequence, including its distinct prologue but excluding the repeated full Erat homo homily.

locators.value: sequence fols. 215v-227v, canvases 318-342; full Erat homo at fols. 221r-222r omitted
parallel sequence fols. 19v(a)-30v(a); current catalog appears to omit the Exaltation of the Cross homily

content_parts.label: 1. Nisi granum: grain-of-wheat prologue
content_parts.summary: A 251-line prologue on Christ as the grain of wheat, the Passion, Eucharistic bread, judgment, and salvation; Horstmann treats it as distinct from the feast sequence proper.

content_parts.label: 2. Stabat Johannes: Vigil of Saint Andrew
content_parts.summary: A 190-line vigil homily beginning Stabat Johannes, in two verse groups.

content_parts.label: 3. Ambulans Jesus: Saint Andrew
content_parts.summary: A 250-line homily for Saint Andrew beginning Ambulans Jesus, in two verse groups.

content_parts.label: 4. Liber generationis: Conception of the Virgin
content_parts.summary: A 182-line homily for the Conception of the Virgin beginning Liber generationis.

content_parts.label: 5. Thomas unus: Saint Thomas the Apostle
content_parts.summary: A 208-line homily for Saint Thomas the Apostle beginning Thomas unus.

content_parts.label: 6. Dicebat Jesus: Saint Stephen
content_parts.summary: A 382-line homily for Saint Stephen beginning Dicebat Jesus.

content_parts.label: 7. Dixit Jesus Petro: Saint John the Apostle
content_parts.summary: A 184-line homily for Saint John the Apostle beginning Dixit Jesus Petro.

content_parts.label: 8. Angelus Domini apparuit: Holy Innocents
content_parts.summary: A 332-line homily for the Holy Innocents beginning Angelus Domini apparuit.

content_parts.label: 9. Nisi granum: Saint Thomas Becket
content_parts.summary: A ninety-six-line homily for Saint Thomas Becket beginning Nisi granum.

content_parts.label: 10. Ecce nos relinquimus: Conversion of Saint Paul
content_parts.summary: A 218-line homily for the Conversion of Saint Paul beginning Ecce nos relinquimus.

content_parts.label: 11. Venit Jesus: Chair of Saint Peter
content_parts.summary: A 225-line homily for the Chair of Saint Peter beginning Venit Jesus.
notes: A title-only Purification pointer precedes this full homily in the manuscript sequence and is not counted as a separate content part.

content_parts.label: 12. Confitebor tibi: Saint Matthias
content_parts.summary: A 114-line homily for Saint Matthias beginning Confitebor tibi.

content_parts.label: 13. Non turbetur: Saints Philip and James
content_parts.summary: A 128-line homily for Saints Philip and James beginning Non turbetur.
notes: A title-only Annunciation pointer precedes this homily. The full Erat homo Invention of the Cross homily follows it in both manuscripts but is omitted from Horstmann's selection; a further title-only pointer for John before the Latin Gate then precedes part 14.

content_parts.label: 14. Hoc est preceptum: Vigil of Saints Peter and Paul
content_parts.summary: A 224-line vigil homily for Saints Peter and Paul beginning Hoc est preceptum.

content_parts.label: 15. Jussit Herodes: Octave of Saints Peter and Paul
content_parts.summary: A 164-line homily for the Octave of Saints Peter and Paul beginning Jussit Herodes.
notes: A title-only Saints Peter and Paul pointer precedes this full octave homily.

content_parts.label: 16. Simile est regnum: Saint Margaret
content_parts.summary: A 138-line homily for Saint Margaret beginning Simile est regnum; p. 114 ends with the article continuation notice.

content_parts.label: 17. Rogabat Jesus: Saint Mary Magdalene
content_parts.summary: A 384-line Mary Magdalene homily with six separately headed exempla; lines 23-100 parallel Horstmann's first 1877 Gospel story.

content_parts.label: 18. Accessit mater: Saint James the Greater
content_parts.summary: A 144-line homily for Saint James the Greater beginning Accessit mater.

content_parts.label: 19. Intravit Jesus: Assumption of the Virgin
content_parts.summary: A 116-line homily for the Assumption of the Virgin beginning Intravit Jesus.
notes: A title-only Lawrence pointer precedes this full homily in the manuscript sequence.

content_parts.label: 20. Facta est contentio: Saint Bartholomew
content_parts.summary: A 214-line homily for Saint Bartholomew beginning Facta est contentio, in three verse groups.

content_parts.label: 21. Nunc iudicium: Exaltation of the Holy Cross
content_parts.summary: A 162-line homily for the Exaltation of the Holy Cross beginning Nunc iudicium.
notes: A title-only Nativity of the Virgin pointer precedes this homily. Current Add MS 22283 catalog structure has no corresponding Nunc iudicium item and moves directly to Matthew at fol. 28r; verify against images before treating it as a definitive witness lacuna.

content_parts.label: 22. Vidit Jesus hominem sedentem: Saint Matthew
content_parts.summary: A 172-line homily for Saint Matthew beginning Vidit Jesus hominem sedentem.

content_parts.label: 23. Quis putas maior: Saint Michael
content_parts.summary: A 212-line homily for Saint Michael beginning Quis putas maior.

content_parts.label: 24. Designavit Dominus: Saint Luke
content_parts.summary: A 160-line homily for Saint Luke beginning Designavit Dominus.

content_parts.label: 25. Hec mando vobis: Saints Simon and Jude
content_parts.summary: A 216-line homily for Saints Simon and Jude beginning Hec mando vobis.

content_parts.label: 26. Videns Jesus turbas: All Saints
content_parts.summary: A 180-line homily for All Saints beginning Videns Jesus turbas.

content_parts.label: 27. Vos estis sal terre: for a Doctor or Confessor
content_parts.summary: A 140-line common homily for a doctor or confessor, tentatively associated by Horstmann with Saint Martin, in three verse groups.
notes: The manuscript itself does not supply a feast label; avoid presenting Saint Martin as certain.

content_parts.label: 28. Egressus Jesus: Dedication of a Church
content_parts.summary: A 196-line homily for the dedication of a church beginning Egressus Jesus, in three verse groups.
notes: The ending canvas and folio also begin the separate following work How a Man Shall Live Perfectly; the cue after Amen is not part of this homily.

related_works.title: The Northern Homily Cycle
notes: The target is supplementary feast-day material in the Vernon/Simeon first expansion, not the original early-fourteenth-century northern cycle as a whole.

related_works.title: Erat homo (Invention of the Cross homily; repeated Temporale homily 52)
notes: A complete homily at Vernon fols. 221r-222r and Add MS 22283 fols. 24v(b)-25r(a). Horstmann retains only its incipit/reference position and excludes the full repeated text from CME00064.

related_works.title: How a Man Shall Live Perfectly
notes: The separate following work begins on the shared final leaf, Vernon fol. 227v and Add MS 22283 fol. 30v(a); its printed cue after the final Amen is outside CME00064.

related_works.title: Die Evangelien-Geschichten der Homiliensammlung des Ms. Vernon
notes: Part 17 lines 23-100 parallel the first Gospel story in Horstmann's 1877 edition represented by CME00062; this is not a whole-work derivation.

evidence.citation: Kyvero-Press/CME, CME00064 XML at commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for title, responsibility, encoded languages, twenty-eight divisions, individual incipits and headings, 5,582 lines, sixty-four verse groups, page spans, notes, and revision history.
Immediate edition for exact title, editor/transcriber role, twenty-eight-item text, Latin rubrics, German apparatus, line and page spans, six internal pointers, 1877 parallel, and article boundaries.

evidence.citation: Carl Horstmann, Altenglische Legenden, Neue Folge (1881), pp. LXXIV-LXXVII.
evidence.summary: Primary historical discussion of the feast order, prologue distinction, regional and authorship hypotheses, full manuscript sequence, six pointers, Erat homo, and Vernon/Add relationship claim.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Vernon catalog, complete IIIF manifest, transcription annotations, and item-by-item image mapping.
evidence.summary: Confirms witness date and tentative origin, target at fols. 215v-227v, all twenty-eight item spans, omitted full Erat homo, six title-only pointers, and shared boundary leaves.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts, Add MS 22283, record 032-002034756.
evidence.summary: Confirms parallel sequence at fols. 19v(a)-30v(a), Add Scribe 1 execution, codex date and tentative north-Worcestershire origin, individual item spans, and current apparent absence of Nunc iudicium.

evidence.citation: Anne B. Thompson, ed., The Northern Homily Cycle, Middle English Texts Series (2008), online introduction.
evidence.summary: Current scholarly basis for homily form and for treating Vernon/Simeon as the first major late-fourteenth-century, Midland expansion adding feast-day material, without transferring the original cycle's northern date/place to these additions.

evidence.citation: Wendy Scase and Nick Kennedy, complete Vernon facsimile/transcription dataset, Oxford University Research Archive.
evidence.summary: Confirms availability of a complete modern facsimile and transcription supporting future verification of manuscript forms and editorial expansions.

open_questions.question: Can modern scholarship refine or confirm Horstmann's distinct northwestern-prologue and southern-homilist authorship/localization hypotheses?
next_steps: Review current scholarship devoted specifically to the Vernon/Simeon first expansion and Proprium sequence
Keep dialect region, composition place, and manuscript production place distinct

open_questions.question: Does Add MS 22283 truly lack the Nunc iudicium Exaltation of the Cross homily, or only a current catalog entry for it?
next_steps: Inspect complete images around fols. 27v-28r when available
Ask the British Library to confirm the sequence
Do not supply an Add locator for part 21 until verified

open_questions.question: Which precise feast was intended for the Vos estis sal terre doctor/confessor homily?
next_steps: Review liturgical and manuscript scholarship before normalizing the item to Saint Martin
Retain a common-feast label with the Martin association marked tentative

open_questions.question: What exact middle and end fragments did the 2016 U-M revision remove?
next_steps: Compare earlier encoding snapshots or proofsheets
Do not infer that the unspecified removals are Erat homo or How a Man Shall Live Perfectly

open_questions.question: Did Horstmann explicitly define all embedded italics as abbreviation expansions?
next_steps: Compare individual forms with the complete Vernon images and Scase transcription

notes: Feast labels are editorial identifications: Horstmann states that the manuscript itself does not name the feasts.
The six title-only references belong to manuscript navigation and are retained in the edition, but they are not counted as independent full homilies in this content structure.
Erat homo is a complete homily in both Vernon and Add MS 22283, not a title-only reference. Its full text is absent because Horstmann editorially omitted the repeated Temporale homily.
Part 21 has no Add MS 22283 locator because the current catalog appears to omit it; absence awaits image verification.
The article's correct target pagination is 83-114 and 299-321. Printed pp. 322-326 are unrelated reviews and are not part of this work.
The work is verse for cataloging purposes. Brief German editorial prose and Latin rubrics are language/editorial layers, not evidence that the medieval homilies are mixed prose and verse.
